title: Step 2: Update the GCP Key details.

sections.heading: Enable Customer-Managed Keys with Google Cloud KMS
sections.body:
Customer key management in Atlas follows a process called envelope encryption.
This process creates multiple layers of encryption by encrypting one key with another
key. To enable customer key management, Atlas uses the following encryption keys:

sections.heading: Example
sections.body:
Consider the following encryption hierarchy for a three-node replica set.
Atlas uses the CMK (customer-managed key) from Google Cloud KMS to encrypt a unique
MongoDB Master Key for each node in the cluster. Each node also contains
three databases, each of which is encrypted with a unique
per-database encryption key. When the cluster starts up,
Atlas decrypts the MongoDB Master Key by using the CMK (customer-managed key) from
Google Cloud KMS and supplies this to the MongoDB Server.

> **Note:** If you revoke Atlas's access to the CMK (customer-managed key), Atlas shuts down the
> nodes in your cluster and you can't access your data until
> you restore access to the CMK (customer-managed key).

![Diagram of CMK workflow with Google Cloud KMS and Atlas](/images/gcp-kms-cmk-diagram.png)

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To configure customer key management, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>)
access to the project.

Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves to the
project as a `Project Owner`.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
To enable customer-managed keys with Google Cloud KMS (Key Management Service) for a MongoDB
project, you must have:

- Use an M10 or larger cluster.

* Your Google Cloud Service Account Key.

* Your symmetric Encryption Key in Google Cloud KMS.

* The Key Version Resource ID associated with your Google Cloud KMS
  Encryption Key.

* A Google Cloud service account with credentials specified in your Service
  Account Key with sufficient permissions to:

  - Get the Google Cloud KMS Encryption Key version.

  - Encrypt data with the Google Cloud KMS Encryption Key version.

  - Decrypt data with the Google Cloud KMS Encryption Key.

  > **Note:** The key, not the key version, handles decryption.

* If your Google Cloud KMS (Key Management Service) configuration requires it, create [Access
  Levels from GCP](https://cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/use-access-levels)
  for [Atlas IP addresses](atlas-add-inbound-ips) and the public
  IP addresses or DNS hostnames of your cluster nodes so that Atlas
  can communicate with your KMS (Key Management Service). If the node IP addresses
  [Do Atlas cluster's public IPs ever change?](faq-public-ip-changes), you must update your
  configuration to avoid connectivity interruptions.

> **See Also:** See the Google Cloud documentation to learn how to:
>
> - [Create a service account key](https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/getting-started).
>
> - [Obtain a key version resource ID](https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/object-hierarchy#key_version).
>
> - [Grant roles to service accounts](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/granting-roles-to-service-accounts).

sections.heading: Enable Customer-Managed Keys for a Project
sections.body:
You must enable customer key management for a project before you can
enable it on a cluster in that project.

Enter the key version resource ID for the Google Cloud KMS key
that you want to use to encrypt your data at rest.

Your key version resource ID is the fully-qualified resource name
for a [CryptoKeyVersion](https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.keyRings.cryptoKeys.cryptoKeyVersions#CryptoKeyVersion).

> **Important:** You must enter a valid ID before you can configure your
> authentication method.

Atlas supports two authentication methods for GCP KMS:

- **Service Account (Recommended)**: Use an Atlas-managed
  service account specific to your project to authenticate to GCP.

- **Static Credentials**: Provide a customer-managed service account key.

Service Account

First-time Setup

If you have not yet set up a service account for your Atlas project:

- Click Authorize a new service account.
  The Encrypt Data with GCP KMS dialog box appears.

- Click the Set Up button to create your GCP resources.
  Atlas provisions the GCP [folder](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/creating-managing-folders)
  and creates the service account. The folder is required in order to
  create service accounts.

- Select the Atlas service account from the dropdown menu
  or [create a new one](create-org-api-key).

- Run the [gcloud](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install) commands
  that appear to grant the service account the following
  required permissions:

  - Enable Encrypt/Decrypt operations

    ```sh
    gcloud kms keys add-iam-policy-binding \
      <key-name> \
        --location <location> \
        --keyring <keyring-name> \
        --member <ATLAS_OWNED_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL> \
        --role="roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter"
    ```

  - Enable GetPublicKey operations

    ```sh
    gcloud kms keys add-iam-policy-binding \
      <key-name> \
        --location <location> \
        --keyring <keyring-name> \
        --member <ATLAS_OWNED_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL> \
        --role="roles/cloudkms.viewer"
    ```

- Click Validate & Finish to confirm.

Existing Service Account

If you have an existing service account for your Atlas project:

- Click Authorize a new service account.
  The Encrypt Data with GCP KMS dialog box appears.

- Select your existing
  Atlas service account from the dropdown menu
  or [create a new one](create-org-api-key).

- Run the [gcloud](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install) commands
  that appear to grant the service account the following
  required permissions:

  - Enable Encrypt/Decrypt operations

    ```sh
    gcloud kms keys add-iam-policy-binding \
      <key-name> \
        --location <location> \
        --keyring <keyring-name> \
        --member <ATLAS_OWNED_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL> \
        --role="roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter"
    ```

  - Enable GetPublicKey operations

    ```sh
    gcloud kms keys add-iam-policy-binding \
      <key-name> \
        --location <location> \
        --keyring <keyring-name> \
        --member <ATLAS_OWNED_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL> \
        --role="roles/cloudkms.viewer"
    ```

- Click Validate & Finish to confirm.

Static Credentials

sections.heading: Enable Customer-Managed Keys for a Project
sections.body:
To grant access in a secure manner, Atlas uses
a [service account](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-impersonation)
specific to your Atlas project to authenticate to GCP.
You can then assign policies to the service
account to control access to your project's resources.

> **Note:** If you previously configured encryption at rest with static credentials
> and are now migrating to service account-based authentication,
> note that this migration is strictly one-way. Atlas does not
> support reverting back to the static credential authentication method.

If you have an existing Atlas GCP service account with the
required permissions already configured, select it from the
Connect Service Account dropdown menu. Otherwise,
complete the following steps:

sections.heading: Enable Customer-Managed Keys for a Project
sections.body:
You can manually specify a service account key by toggling
Authorize with static credentials to On. Then, enter your key
formatted as a JSON object with the encryption credentials for
your GCP service account in the text box.

> **Important:** Static credentials will be deprecated in a future release.
> Use service account authentication for new projects
> when possible. Existing projects using static credentials can
> continue to use them but cannot switch back once migrated to
> service account authentication.

sections.heading: Enable Customer Key Management for an Atlas Cluster
sections.body:
After you [Enable Customer-Managed Keys for a Project](gcp-kms-configure-project), you must enable customer key
management for each Atlas cluster that contains data that you want
to encrypt.

> **Note:** You must have the [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) role to
> enable customer key management for clusters in that project.

For new clusters, toggle the
[Manage Your Own Encryption Keys](create-cluster-enable-encryption)
setting to **Yes** when you create the cluster.

For existing clusters:

For the cluster that contains data that you want to encrypt, click the
, then select Edit Configuration.

- Expand the Additional Settings panel.

- Toggle the Manage your own encryption keys setting to
  Yes.

- Verify the status of the Require Private Networking
  setting for your cluster.

  If you configured Encryption at Rest Using CMK (customer-managed key) (Over Private
  Networking) for Atlas at the project level, the status is
  Active. If you haven't configured any private
  endpoint connection for your project, the status is
  Inactive.

* Click Review Changes.

* Review your changes, then click Apply Changes to update
  your cluster.

sections.heading: Alerts
sections.body:
Atlas automatically creates an [encryption key rotation alert](<GCP encryption key elapsed time since last rotation is above (n) days>)
once you configure customer key management for a project. You can reset this alert at any time by
[rotating your GCP Key Version Resource ID](gcp-kms-rotate-key).

sections.heading: Rotate your GCP Key Version Resource ID
sections.body:
> **Note:** This feature is not available for any of the following deployments:
>
> - Free clusters
>
> - Flex clusters
>
> To learn more, see [Limits](limits).

sections.heading: MongoDB Master Key - MongoDB Responsibility
sections.body:
When you use your own cloud provider KMS (Key Management Service), Atlas automatically rotates
MongoDB Master Keys at least every 90 days. Your key rotation will begin
during a [Configure Maintenance Window](configure-maintenance-window),
if you have one configured. Deferring maintenance (either manually or automatically)
may cause the key to be rotated past the 90-day mark.
Keys are rotated on a
rolling basis and the process does not require the data to be rewritten.

sections.heading: Your Google Cloud CMK (customer-managed key) - Your Responsibility
sections.body:
Atlas does *not* automatically rotate the Key Version Resource ID
used for Google Cloud key management.

As a best practice, Atlas creates an [alert](<AWS encryption key elapsed time since last rotation is above (n) days>) to remind you
to rotate your GCP Key Version Resource ID every 90 days by default when
you [Google Cloud Platform Key Management Service](security-gcp-kms) for the
Atlas project. You can configure the time period of this alert.

You can [rotate](https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/rotate-key) your
Google Cloud CMK (customer-managed key) yourself or configure your Google Cloud KMS (Key Management Service) instance to
[automatically rotate](https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/rotate-key#automatic)
your CMK (customer-managed key). If you configure automatic Google Cloud CMK (customer-managed key) rotation, the default
time period for rotation is approximately 365 days.

If you have already set up an automatic CMK (customer-managed key) rotation in Google Cloud and
don't want to receive the Atlas alert to rotate your CMK (customer-managed key) every 90
days, you can [Configure Alert Settings](configure-alerts) the default alert period
to be greater than 365 days.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
You must create a new Service Account Key in the Google Cloud account
associated with your Atlas project.

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
The following procedure documents how to rotate your Atlas project Key Identifier by specifying a new Key Version Resource ID in Atlas.

- Click Google Cloud KMS if the
  Google Cloud KMS tab is not already active.

- Expand Encryption Key Credentials if the
  Encryption Key Credentials dialog box is not already
  displayed.

- Enter the GCP Key Version Resource ID in the
  Key Identifier entry.

  Include the fully-qualified resource name for a
  [CryptoKeyVersion](https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.keyRings.cryptoKeys.cryptoKeyVersions#CryptoKeyVersion).

  > **Example:** 
  >
  > ```sh
  > projects/my-project-0/locations/us-east4/keyRings/my-key-ring-0/cryptoKeys/my-key-0/cryptoKeyVersions/1
  > ```

  The encryption key **must** belong to the Google Cloud Service Account Key
  configured for your Atlas project. Click the Service
  Account Key section to view the currently configured Service
  Account Key for the project.

- Click Update Credentials.

Atlas displays a banner in the Atlas console during the
Key Identifier rotation process.

> **Warning:** Do not delete or disable the original Key Version Resource ID until
> your changes have deployed.

If the cluster uses [Cloud Backup](backup-cloud-provider), do **not** delete
or disable the original Key Version Resource ID until you
ensure that no snapshots used that key for encryption.

sections.heading: Alerts
sections.body:
Atlas resets the
[encryption key rotation alert](<GCP encryption key elapsed time since last rotation is above (n) days>)
timer at the completion of this procedure.

sections.heading: Enable Customer-Managed Keys for Search Nodes
sections.body:
By default, MongoDB and [search processes](about-mongot) run on the same nodes.
With this architecture, customer-managed encryption applies to your database data,
but it does not apply to search indexes.

When you enable [Search Nodes](what-is-search-node), search processes run on separate nodes. This allows
you to enable [Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption),
so you can encrypt both database data and search indexes with the same
customer-managed keys for comprehensive encryption coverage.

> **Note:** Database nodes and Search Nodes use different encryption methods
> with the same customer-managed keys. Database nodes use the
> [WiredTiger Encrypted Storage Engine](manual/core/security-encryption-at-rest),
> while Search Nodes use encryption at the disk level.

> **Important:** 
>
> This feature is available across KMS (Key Management Service) providers, but the
> Search Nodes must be on AWS (Amazon Web Services).

sections.heading: Related Topics
sections.body:
- To enable Encryption at Rest using your Key Management when deploying
  an Atlas cluster, see [Manage Your Own Encryption Keys](create-cluster-enable-encryption).

- To enable Encryption at Rest using your Key Management for an
  existing Atlas cluster, see
  [Encryption at Rest](scale-cluster-enable-encryption).

- To learn more about Encryption at Rest using your Key Management in
  Atlas, see [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption).

- To learn more about MongoDB Encryption at Rest, see
  [Encryption at Rest](manual/core/security-encryption-at-rest) in
  the MongoDB server documentation.

- To learn more about Encryption at Rest with Cloud Backups, see
  [Storage Engine and Cloud Backup Encryption](encrypted-cloud-provider-snapshot).
